items.title: Keep PRs small and focused
items.prefer: Sub-400-line PRs scoped to one concern (refactor / feature / fix).
items.why: Review quality drops sharply above ~400 lines; small PRs ship faster and break less.
items.example: Split a big refactor into: 1) extract helper (no behaviour change), 2) call it from N places, 3) delete the old code.

items.title: Use a PR template
items.prefer: Commit `.github/pull_request_template.md` with What / Why / How / Test plan headers.
items.why: Forces authors to articulate intent; reviewers spend zero time reverse-engineering.
items.example: Even a 5-line PR deserves a 3-line description.

items.title: Mark comment intent: blocking / suggestion / nit
items.prefer: Prefix every non-blocking comment with `[suggestion]` or `[nit]`.
items.why: Authors know what they MUST fix vs what's optional; turnaround time halves.
items.example: `[nit] could be a one-liner`. `[blocking] this misses the empty-list case`.

items.title: Configure branch protection on `main`
items.avoid: PRs as suggestion-only on important branches.
items.prefer: Require: PR, ≥1 approval, green CI checks, no force-push, no direct push.
items.why: Configured policy replaces tribal discipline; small teams benefit most.
items.example: GitHub: Settings → Branches → Add rule for `main`.

items.title: Use CODEOWNERS to auto-route reviews
items.why: Reviews land with the right expertise instantly; reduces 'who owns this?' delay.
